name: National Security Council
description: The National Security Council (NSC) is the principal forum used by the President of the United States for considering national security and foreign policy matters with senior national security advisors and cabinet officials. It is part of the Executive Office of the President. Established in 1947 by the National Security Act, the NSC advises and assists the President on national security and foreign policies. Conspiracy theories surrounding the NSC often portray it as a shadow government, wielding undue influence over US foreign policy and domestic affairs, operating outside of public scrutiny and accountability. Proponents of these theories point to the NSC's broad mandate, its role in covert operations, and the secrecy surrounding its deliberations as evidence of its hidden power. They suggest the NSC manipulates events to further a hidden agenda, often linked to globalist or corporatist interests. Mainstream perspectives view the NSC as a necessary institution for coordinating national security policy within the Executive Branch. While acknowledging the need for some secrecy in national security matters, academics and government officials emphasize the NSC's advisory role and its subordination to presidential authority and Congressional oversight. Critics of conspiracy theories argue that they often exaggerate the NSC's power and misinterpret its functions, ignoring the checks and balances built into the US political system. The NSC's structure and processes are subject to change depending on the president in office, impacting the level of power it holds.
subtitle: US National Security Policy Coordinator

claims.claim: The NSC is a shadow government controlling US policy.
claims.evidence: Secrecy of meetings, involvement in covert operations.
claims.criticism: Exaggerates influence, ignores checks and balances, advisory role.

claims.claim: The NSC manipulates events to serve a globalist agenda.
claims.evidence: Involvement in regime change operations, promotion of free trade agreements.
claims.criticism: Oversimplifies complex geopolitical factors, misinterprets policy motivations.

claims.claim: The NSC operates without public accountability.
claims.evidence: Limited transparency, lack of Congressional oversight in some areas.
claims.criticism: Subject to presidential authority, Congressional oversight through budget and legislation, classified information protections.

keyFigures.name: Oliver North
keyFigures.role: NSC staffer involved in the Iran-Contra affair

keyFigures.name: Zbigniew Brzezinski
keyFigures.role: National Security Advisor under President Carter, influential strategist

keyFigures.name: Amy Zegart
keyFigures.role: Academic specializing in intelligence and national security, critical of NSC overreach

mythologyConnections: Connections to the idea of a hidden hand guiding events.
Parallels to ancient councils of advisors to rulers, often shrouded in secrecy.

timeline.event: National Security Act establishes the National Security Council.
NSC staff involved in illegal arms sales and funding of Contra rebels.
Expanded role of the NSC in coordinating homeland security and counterterrorism efforts.

sources.author: Ivo H. Daalder, I. M. Destler
sources.work: In the Shadow of the Oval Office: The President and National Security Policy
sources.title: Ivo H. Daalder, I. M. Destler

sources.author: David Rothkopf
sources.work: Running the World: The Inside Story of the National Security Council and the Architects of American Power
sources.title: David Rothkopf

sources.author: Various Authors
sources.work: Numerous conspiracy theory websites and books
